text: The surgeon performs dissection to separate the small bowel adhesions from the pelvic region, using careful maneuvers to free the bowel from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon performs a pelvic lymph node dissection, revealing the small bowel adhered laterally within the pelvic region.
The surgeon identifies and dissects through the visible tissue planes, noting that the adhesions in the area are minimal.
The surgeon identifies the planned line of dissection, clearly visible amidst the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies the gonadal vessels on the right side, which are medialized, visible adjacent to the surgical field.
A sponge is placed to hold the small bowel aside, and the surgeon begins the medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon dissects around the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, preparing to take the superior hemorrhoidal artery.

The surgeon dissects along the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to visualize the bifurcation at the takeoff of the left colic artery. The left colic artery is preserved while the superior hemorrhoidal artery is prepared for ligation.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection, using a sponge to keep the small bowel out of the surgical field.
The surgeon places a sponge to protect the retroperitoneum before proceeding with the lateral dissection.

The surgeon marks the planned proximal margin on the bowel, which appears relatively normal, to ensure accurate identification post-mobilization. The surgeon follows the white line, aligning with the medial dissection plane.
The surgeon demonstrates a portion of the lateral dissection, focusing on the separation of tissue layers along the lateral aspect of the colon.
The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, demonstrating the selective dissection technique used to free this section of the colon.

The surgeon completes the dissection of pelvic adhesions, which are visibly associated with diverticular disease and previous hysterectomy. The surgeon then marks the potential planned distal margin and briefly returns to mobilize the splenic flexure.

The surgeon assesses the reach of the bowel, noting that it extends deep past the promontory. The proximal margin is taken, and the mesentery is dissected up to this margin while preserving the left colic artery.
The surgeon cleans the distal margin of the colon, preparing it for resection.
The surgeon checks perfusion using indocyanine green (ICG) fluorescence at the margin and proceeds to transect the tissue using scissors.
The surgeon checks the distal margin and proceeds to divide the tissue at this location.

The surgeon separates the mesentery from the colon specimen to facilitate transrectal extraction. The colon and mesentery are removed as two separate specimens. Instruments are used to hold open the mouth of the rectum, aiding in the extraction process.
The surgeon repositions the tissue and begins sewing a two-layer end-to-end anastomosis, using sutures to connect the two ends of the bowel.

The surgeon checks the ICG fluorescence to assess perfusion at the surgical site. Using a two-layer posterior lip double suture (LPDS) technique, the surgeon takes bites approximately five millimeters from the cut edge to achieve inversion of the posterior lip.
The surgeon begins sewing the inner layer of the anastomosis, starting in the middle and progressing towards the patient's left side using nine-inch 3-0 V-Loc sutures.

The surgeon begins suturing down the anterior inner row, taking care not to close over the lumen to maintain visibility of the midline for the next stitch. The suture is stopped at a specific point before starting the next suture in the midline and extending to the patient's right.
The surgeon transitions to a canal suture technique as they approach the near corner of the surgical site.

The surgeon joins two sutures in the midline by alternating between them, ensuring complete inversion of the tissue towards the center. The sutures are then tied together, followed by an anterior outer suture using PDS material.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis, observing no tension or bubbles. A 19mm proctoscope is successfully passed through the anastomosis.
